events.text: The patient is a 63-year-old female named Margaret Okonkwo. She has a history of hypertension managed with amlodipine and a remote 20 pack-year smoking history (quit 15 years prior). She works as a retired schoolteacher. Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status at diagnosis: 1. She has no family history of renal cell carcinoma or hereditary cancer syndromes.

At age 63, the patient had a CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast (initial staging), which showed a large heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 11.4 x 9.7 x 10.2 cm arising from the upper and mid poles of the right kidney, with areas of central necrosis. There was extension into the perinephric fat. Multiple enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes were identified, the largest measuring 2.8 x 2.1 cm at the level of the renal hilum and an additional 1.9 cm paraaortic node. The right renal vein appeared patent. No adrenal involvement. No evidence of inferior vena cava thrombus. Radiographic cancer status: cancer present with regional lymphadenopathy.

At age 63, the patient had a CT chest with contrast (initial staging), which showed multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic disease. The largest lesion was in the right lower lobe measuring 1.8 x 1.5 cm. Additional nodules in the left upper lobe (0.9 cm), right upper lobe (0.7 cm), and left lower lobe (0.6 cm). No pleural effusion. No mediastinal adenopathy. Radiographic cancer status: metastatic pulmonary disease confirmed.

At age 63, the patient had a bone scan (initial staging), which showed no definitive evidence of osseous metastatic disease. There was mild degenerative changes in the lumbar spine and bilateral hips, not suspicious for metastasis. Radiographic cancer status: no osseous metastases.

At age 63 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition) clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) of the right kidney. Primary site: right kidney (ICD-O-3 site C64.1). Histology: renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type (ICD-O-3 histology code 8310/3). TNM staging: pT3aNxM1 (radiographic N1 retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy; M1 bilateral pulmonary metastases). ISUP/WHO nuclear grade: Grade 3. Sarcomatoid differentiation: present, estimated 20% of tumor volume. Tumor size: 11.4 cm greatest dimension. Extension into perinephric fat: present. Adrenal involvement: absent. Renal vein invasion: absent. IVC involvement: absent. Lymphovascular invasion: present. IMDC (International Metastatic RCC Database Consortium) risk score: Intermediate risk (2 factors: KPS <80% equivalent due to ECOG 1, and diagnosis to systemic therapy <1 year). Confirmation: positive histology by percutaneous core needle biopsy. Tumor site description: right kidney, upper and mid pole. Summary stage: distant metastasis.

At age 63, the patient had a pathology result from a CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of the right renal mass. Specimen type: core needle biopsy, right kidney mass. Gross description: four tan-white cores measuring 1.0 to 1.5 cm in length. Microscopic findings: renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type, ISUP/WHO nuclear grade 3. The tumor cells have abundant clear cytoplasm with distinct cell borders arranged in nested and alveolar patterns separated by a delicate sinusoidal vascular network. Approximately 20% of the sampled tumor demonstrates sarcomatoid differentiation with high-grade spindle cell morphology, marked nuclear pleomorphism, and brisk mitotic activity. Areas of geographic necrosis are present. Perinephric fat focally involved. Immunohistochemistry: tumor cells positive for CA9 (carbonic anhydrase IX), PAX8, vimentin, and CD10. Negative for CK7, TFE3, and CAIX focal loss. PD-L1 expression: combined positive score (CPS) 15 (Dako 22C3 assay). Final diagnosis: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma, ISUP grade 3 with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation.

At age 63 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for clear cell renal cell carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the right renal mass (core needle biopsy). Platform: FoundationOne CDx (Foundation Medicine). Tumor mutational burden: 5 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). Loss of heterozygosity: high genomic LOH (23% of genome). Actionable and clinically relevant alterations: VHL p.R200* (nonsense, pathogenic, biallelic loss via LOH — canonical clear cell RCC driver), PBRM1 frameshift deletion (exon 15, c.2570_2571delAG, loss-of-function — chromatin remodeling tumor suppressor), BAP1 p.W136* (nonsense, loss-of-function — associated with sarcomatoid differentiation and poor prognosis), SETD2 p.K1740fs*2 (frameshift, loss-of-function — histone methyltransferase), TP53 p.R248W (hotspot missense, gain-of-function — associated with sarcomatoid features). Copy number alterations: chromosome 3p loss (encompassing VHL, PBRM1, BAP1, SETD2), MYC gain (chromosome 8q), CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion (chromosome 9p21). Additional variants of uncertain significance: ARID1A p.S1235L, KDM5C p.R726Q (likely benign), KMT2C p.R668H. No MTOR pathway alterations detected. No actionable fusions identified. No HER2 amplification. Germline testing recommended given BAP1 alteration (patient declined germline follow-up at this time).

At age 63, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board assessment. The case was reviewed by medical oncology, urology, and radiation oncology. Given the large right renal mass (11.4 cm), retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary metastases, the consensus recommendation was for cytoreductive nephrectomy (CN) given IMDC intermediate risk status, good performance status, predominance of disease in the primary site, and the presence of sarcomatoid differentiation warranting tissue for full molecular characterization. Following recovery from surgery, first-line immunotherapy with ipilimumab plus nivolumab (Checkmate 214 regimen) was recommended given intermediate IMDC risk and presence of sarcomatoid differentiation (which is associated with enhanced IO benefit). Cancer present. No response assessment yet — pre-treatment.

At age 63 years, the patient underwent a right radical nephrectomy (cytoreductive nephrectomy) with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection via open approach. Operative findings: right kidney with a large upper/mid pole mass with perinephric fat involvement. Multiple enlarged hilar and paraaortic lymph nodes were resected. The right adrenal gland was preserved. Right renal vein was intact without thrombus. IVC was inspected and free of tumor. Estimated blood loss: 650 mL. Intraoperative blood transfusion: 2 units packed red blood cells. Surgical margins: negative (R0). Procedure intent: cytoreductive (palliative).

At age 63, the patient had a final surgical pathology result from the right radical nephrectomy specimen. Specimen: right kidney with perinephric fat and retroperitoneal lymph nodes. Gross: kidney measuring 14.5 x 11.8 x 9.0 cm, with a dominant right renal mass 11.4 cm in greatest dimension with central necrosis (approximately 30% of tumor area) and yellow-tan solid viable tumor at periphery. Microscopic: renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type, ISUP/WHO nuclear grade 3. Sarcomatoid differentiation present in 20% of sampled tumor (high-grade spindle cell morphology, necrosis). Extension into perinephric fat: present. Gerota's fascia: not involved. Renal sinus fat: focally involved. Renal vein margin: negative. Ureteral margin: negative. Adrenal gland: not submitted (preserved). Lymph nodes: 5 of 8 retroperitoneal lymph nodes positive for metastatic clear cell RCC (5/8). Largest positive node: 2.8 cm with extranodal extension. Final pathologic stage: pT3a pN1 M1 (AJCC 8th edition, Stage IV). Immunohistochemistry on surgical specimen consistent with prior biopsy. PD-L1 CPS on surgical specimen: 18 (Dako 22C3).

At age 63 (approximately 5 weeks post-nephrectomy), the patient had a post-surgical oncology clinic visit. She had recovered well from her right radical nephrectomy. Wound healing was intact, no surgical complications noted. Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL pre-operatively), consistent with expected decline after nephrectomy. ECOG performance status: 1. Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL. She was counseled extensively on the immunotherapy regimen of ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks x 4 cycles (induction), followed by nivolumab 480 mg flat dose every 4 weeks (maintenance). She understood the immune-related adverse event (irAE) risks. Treatment to begin next week. Cancer status: present (pulmonary metastases remain; lymph nodes resected). No response assessment.

At age 63 years (approximately 6 weeks post-nephrectomy), the patient initiated first-line systemic therapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for planned 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab maintenance 480 mg IV every 4 weeks. Treatment intent: palliative (first-line metastatic). Regimen basis: Checkmate 214 trial. Pre-medications: standard anti-emetics and corticosteroid premedication per institutional protocol. Baseline labs within acceptable range. Renal function acceptable (eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m2 post-nephrectomy).

At age 63 (after cycle 2 of ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient developed grade 2 immune-related colitis manifesting as increased stool frequency (5-6 loose stools per day above baseline), mild lower abdominal cramping, and no rectal bleeding. Onset approximately 5 weeks after initiation of ipilimumab/nivolumab. Managed with oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with gradual taper over 6 weeks per NCCN immune-related adverse event management guidelines. Infectious workup (C. difficile, stool culture, ova and parasites) negative. Colonoscopy deferred given clinical Grade 2 and response to steroids. Ipilimumab held for one cycle; nivolumab continued. Diarrhea resolved to Grade 1 after 10 days of steroids. Steroids tapered and discontinued over 6 weeks. CTCAE Grade 2.

At age 63 (after completing 4 induction cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient had a restaging oncology assessment. She tolerated the regimen overall, with the episode of Grade 2 immune colitis that resolved. ECOG performance status: 1. She transitioned to nivolumab 480 mg IV monotherapy every 4 weeks as planned maintenance. Interval symptom review: no new pulmonary symptoms, no hemoptysis, no constitutional symptoms. Laboratory values stable. Cancer status: present, response assessment pending imaging.

At age 63 (approximately 4 months after starting ipilimumab/nivolumab, post 4 induction cycles), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (first restaging scan). Chest: right lower lobe lesion decreased from 1.8 cm to 1.0 cm (44% decrease). Left upper lobe nodule decreased from 0.9 cm to 0.5 cm. Right upper lobe nodule stable at 0.7 cm. Left lower lobe nodule stable at 0.6 cm. Abdomen/pelvis: nephrectomy bed without evidence of local recurrence. No new retroperitoneal adenopathy. No new hepatic or adrenal lesions. Overall assessment: mixed response — partial response in right lower lobe and left upper lobe target lesions per RECIST 1.1; stable disease in other pulmonary nodules. Sum of target lesion diameters: decreased by 28% from baseline (partial response by RECIST 1.1 in target lesions). No new lesions. Radiographic cancer status: cancer present, mixed/partial response.

At age 63 (approximately 5 months on therapy), the patient had an oncology office visit for ongoing nivolumab maintenance. Imaging showed mixed/partial response with reduction in dominant pulmonary lesions but stability in smaller nodules. Clinical decision: continue nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks given overall disease control and tolerability. She reported mild fatigue (Grade 1) and occasional grade 1 arthralgias managed with NSAIDs. No gastrointestinal symptoms. ECOG PS: 1. Thyroid function tests: TSH 6.8 mIU/L (elevated from baseline 2.1), free T4 0.7 ng/dL (low), consistent with Grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism. Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily initiated. No nivolumab dose modification. Cancer present, responding.

At age 63 (approximately 5 months on ipilimumab/nivolumab then nivolumab maintenance), the patient developed immune-related hypothyroidism. TSH elevated at 6.8 mIU/L, free T4 low at 0.7 ng/dL. Symptoms: fatigue, mild cold intolerance, and constipation consistent with hypothyroidism. CTCAE Grade 2 immune-related endocrinopathy (hypothyroidism). Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally daily initiated. Nivolumab continued without dose modification. Thyroid function tests to be monitored every 6-8 weeks. No corticosteroids required for hypothyroidism management.

At age 63-64 (approximately 9 months after starting ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (second restaging scan, disease progression assessment). Chest: right lower lobe lesion now measures 2.4 cm (increased from 1.0 cm at prior scan, and above 1.8 cm baseline — unequivocal progression). Left upper lobe nodule increased from 0.5 cm to 1.2 cm. Right upper lobe nodule increased from 0.7 cm to 1.4 cm. Left lower lobe nodule increased from 0.6 cm to 1.1 cm. Two new small pulmonary nodules identified in the right middle lobe (0.5 and 0.6 cm). Abdomen/pelvis: two new small liver lesions, right hepatic lobe, measuring 0.8 and 0.6 cm, with enhancement pattern suspicious for metastatic disease. No new retroperitoneal adenopathy. No bone lesions. Overall assessment: progressive disease by RECIST 1.1 — new lesions (hepatic) and increase in all target pulmonary lesions. Sum of target lesion diameters increased by 62% from nadir. Radiographic cancer status: cancer present, progressive disease.

At age 64 (9 months after initiating first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient had an oncology visit for disease progression assessment. Restaging CT demonstrated progressive pulmonary disease with new hepatic metastases, confirming progression on nivolumab maintenance. The patient remained clinically well with ECOG PS 1, no significant new symptoms. Discussion of second-line therapy options: cabozantinib (VEGFR/MET/AXL inhibitor) as standard second-line after IO-based therapy, supported by METEOR trial data and real-world evidence in IO-refractory ccRCC. Alternative options discussed: lenvatinib plus everolimus, axitinib. Given the presence of sarcomatoid features and MET pathway relevance (MET amplification absent but AXL overexpression common in sarcomatoid tumors), cabozantinib selected as preferred second-line agent. Nivolumab discontinued. Levothyroxine continued for hypothyroidism. Cancer present, progressing.

At age 64 years (9 months after initiating first-line therapy, following confirmed radiographic progression on nivolumab), the patient initiated second-line systemic therapy with cabozantinib 60 mg orally once daily (standard starting dose). Treatment intent: palliative (second-line metastatic). Regimen basis: METEOR trial, with consideration of MET/AXL/VEGFR activity relevant to sarcomatoid histology. Counseled on common TKI-related adverse effects including hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia), hypertension, diarrhea, fatigue, and mucositis. Preemptive measures discussed: thick-soled footwear, urea-based cream, dose-reduction algorithm reviewed.

At age 64 (approximately 3 weeks after starting cabozantinib 60 mg daily), the patient developed Grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome, HFS). Findings: bilateral palmar and plantar erythema, tenderness, peeling, and blistering affecting weight-bearing areas and palmar creases, limiting some instrumental activities of daily living. Management: cabozantinib dose reduced to 40 mg orally daily per dose-reduction algorithm. Urea 40% cream applied twice daily, thick-soled cushioned footwear, and avoidance of heat/friction. The patient was referred to dermatology for supportive care optimization. CTCAE Grade 2 hand-foot skin reaction.

At age 64 (approximately 4 weeks after starting cabozantinib), the patient developed Grade 2 diarrhea with 4-6 loose stools per day above baseline, without blood or significant cramping. Management: loperamide 2 mg after each loose stool (up to 8 doses per day as needed), dietary modifications (low-residue diet, BRAT diet elements), and adequate hydration. Infectious causes excluded. Diarrhea improved to Grade 1 (2-3 stools per day) within 2 weeks on 40 mg cabozantinib. Cabozantinib held briefly for 5 days then resumed at 40 mg. No hospitalization required. CTCAE Grade 2.

At age 64 (6 weeks after starting cabozantinib), the patient was noted to have Grade 2 hypertension on cabozantinib (blood pressure 158/96 mmHg on amlodipine). Amlodipine dose uptitrated from 5 mg to 10 mg daily. Blood pressure adequately controlled to <140/90 mmHg on adjusted regimen. No cabozantinib dose modification required for hypertension. CTCAE Grade 2 hypertension.

At age 64 (approximately 6 weeks after starting cabozantinib 40 mg daily), the patient had an oncology visit. Hand-foot syndrome had improved to Grade 1 with supportive care. Diarrhea controlled at Grade 1 on loperamide PRN. Blood pressure well-controlled on amlodipine 10 mg. She tolerated cabozantinib 40 mg overall. TSH normalized to 2.8 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg. ECOG PS: 1. Restaging imaging planned. She reported feeling generally well with manageable side effects. Cancer present, response assessment pending.

At age 64 (approximately 3 months after starting cabozantinib, 12 months from original diagnosis), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (restaging on cabozantinib). Chest: right lower lobe lesion decreased from 2.4 cm to 1.9 cm. Left upper lobe nodule decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.9 cm. Right upper lobe nodule decreased from 1.4 cm to 1.1 cm. Left lower lobe nodule decreased from 1.1 cm to 0.9 cm. Right middle lobe nodules stable (0.4 and 0.5 cm). Abdomen: hepatic lesions in right lobe stable at 0.8 and 0.6 cm, no new hepatic lesions. No new osseous lesions. No new sites of disease. Overall assessment: stable disease by RECIST 1.1 — minor decreases in some target lesions but no confirmed partial response (sum of target lesion diameters decreased by 18% from pre-cabozantinib baseline, below -30% threshold for PR). No new lesions. Radiographic cancer status: cancer present, stable disease.

At age 64 (3 months on cabozantinib, 12 months from diagnosis), the patient had a scheduled oncology clinic visit reviewing restaging results. CT imaging demonstrated stable disease on cabozantinib 40 mg with minor reductions in pulmonary targets and stable hepatic disease. The patient and oncologist agreed to continue cabozantinib at 40 mg given disease stability, tolerability improvement, and clinical benefit. Ongoing management of hand-foot syndrome (Grade 1), diarrhea (Grade 1), and hypertension (controlled). Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine. Quality of life assessment: moderate, ECOG PS 1. No evidence of clinical deterioration. Patient expressed desire to continue active treatment. Next restaging in 3 months. Cancer present, stable disease on cabozantinib.

At age 64 (approximately 6 months after starting cabozantinib, 15 months from original diagnosis), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (follow-up restaging). Chest: right lower lobe lesion stable at 1.8 cm. Left upper lobe nodule stable at 0.9 cm. Right upper lobe nodule stable at 1.0 cm. Left lower lobe nodule stable at 0.8 cm. Right middle lobe nodules unchanged. Abdomen: hepatic lesions stable at 0.7 and 0.5 cm. No new sites of disease. Nephrectomy bed without recurrence. No new skeletal lesions. Overall assessment: continued stable disease by RECIST 1.1. No progression and no new lesions. Radiographic cancer status: cancer present, stable disease — best overall response stable disease on cabozantinib.

At age 64 (6 months on cabozantinib, 15 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit. Continued stable disease on cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Ongoing toxicities remain manageable: hand-foot syndrome Grade 1 (stable with urea cream and footwear modifications), diarrhea Grade 1 (1-2 stools above baseline, controlled with loperamide PRN), and blood pressure well-controlled. Thyroid function stable on levothyroxine. ECOG PS: 1. Patient reports moderate fatigue, some limitations in walking, but overall functional at home. Nutritional status adequate; weight loss of 4 kg since starting TKI therapy, being monitored. Discussion of continued treatment and goals of care initiated. Patient wishes to continue active therapy. Social work referral placed for psychosocial support. Palliative care consultation discussed; patient not yet interested but agreed to revisit. Cancer present, stable disease.

At age 64 (approximately 18 months from original diagnosis, 9 months on cabozantinib), the patient had a comprehensive oncology visit. Restaging scans (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) show continued stable disease — all target lesions minimally changed from prior scan, no new lesions. Persistent stable disease representing durable benefit from cabozantinib. Performance status remains ECOG 1. Key current issues: (1) Hand-foot syndrome Grade 1, well-managed; patient has adapted lifestyle modifications. (2) Diarrhea Grade 1, managed with dietary adjustments and PRN loperamide. (3) Hypertension Grade 1-2, stable on amlodipine 10 mg. (4) Hypothyroidism from prior immunotherapy, stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg. (5) Mild chronic fatigue, referred to integrative oncology for exercise counseling. Lab: CBC within acceptable range, slight anemia (Hgb 10.8 g/dL), creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (stable), LFTs normal, TSH 3.1 mIU/L. Treatment plan: continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily with ongoing toxicity monitoring. Next restaging in 3 months. Goals of care: active treatment with quality of life optimization. Cancer present, stable disease with ongoing TKI therapy.

documents.text:
CT CHEST WITH CONTRAST — INITIAL STAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Known large right renal mass identified on CT abdomen/pelvis performed for staging workup. Renal mass measures 11.4 x 9.7 x 10.2 cm with perinephric fat extension and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. CT chest obtained to evaluate for pulmonary or mediastinal involvement. 63-year-old female with no prior thoracic malignancy.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest performed with intravenous iodinated contrast material during portal venous phase acquisition. Images reconstructed in axial, sagittal, and coronal planes at 1.25 mm slice thickness. Lung windows, mediastinal windows, and bone windows reviewed.

COMPARISON:
No prior chest imaging available for comparison.

FINDINGS:

Lungs and Airways:
The central airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. There are multiple discrete pulmonary nodules distributed throughout both lungs. The dominant lesion is a well-circumscribed solid nodule in the right lower lobe, posterior basal segment, measuring 1.8 x 1.5 cm in axial dimension. The nodule demonstrates mild heterogeneous attenuation without central cavitation. Additional nodules are identified as follows: a 0.9 cm solid nodule in the left upper lobe apicoposterior segment, a 0.7 cm solid nodule in the right upper lobe posterior segment, and a 0.6 cm solid nodule in the left lower lobe superior segment. The nodules are solid, well-defined, and lack satellite lesions or associated ground-glass opacity. No pulmonary consolidation, lobar collapse, or ground-glass opacities suggestive of pneumonia or inflammatory process. No endobronchial lesion identified. No emphysematous changes are present, which is notable given the patient's remote smoking history.

Pleura:
No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pleural thickening or nodularity. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum:
The mediastinum is midline. Heart size is within normal limits. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without evidence of aneurysm or dissection. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy by size criteria. The largest mediastinal node measures 0.8 cm in short axis, within normal limits. No anterior mediastinal mass.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues:
No chest wall lesions. No subcutaneous nodules or adenopathy in the axillae. Breast tissue symmetric without focal mass.

Bones:
The visualized thoracic vertebral bodies, ribs, sternum, and scapulae are intact without lytic or blastic lesions to suggest osseous involvement. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine are noted. No pathologic fracture.

Upper Abdomen (included on inferior cuts):
Partially visualized right renal mass in the upper to mid pole is again noted, consistent with the large heterogeneous renal mass described on the concurrent abdominal CT. No new upper abdominal finding is identified on these limited cuts.

IMPRESSION:
1. Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules of varying size, the largest in the right lower lobe measuring 1.8 x 1.5 cm, with additional nodules in the left upper lobe (0.9 cm), right upper lobe (0.7 cm), and left lower lobe (0.6 cm). In the clinical context of the known large right renal mass with retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, the distribution, morphology, and multiplicity of these nodules is strongly suspicious for hematogenous pulmonary metastases.
2. No pleural effusion.
3. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathy.
4. No osseous lesions identified on this examination.
5. Correlation with the concurrent abdominal/pelvic staging CT is recommended; multidisciplinary tumor board discussion advised.

MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — POST-OPERATIVE CLINIC NOTE
Department of Medical Oncology

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
REFERRING: Urology / Surgical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old retired schoolteacher presenting to medical oncology approximately 5 weeks following right radical nephrectomy (cytoreductive) with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection for Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC). Visit purpose: post-operative assessment and initiation of first-line systemic immunotherapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo initially presented several months ago with an incidentally noted right renal mass on cross-sectional imaging. Staging workup confirmed a large right-sided heterogeneous renal mass measuring approximately 11.4 cm with extension into the perinephric fat, bilateral pulmonary metastases (largest right lower lobe 1.8 cm), and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (hilar node 2.8 cm, paraaortic 1.9 cm). Staging: pT3a pN1 M1, Stage IV by AJCC 8th edition. CT-guided core needle biopsy confirmed clear cell RCC, ISUP/WHO nuclear grade 3, with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation. PD-L1 CPS was 15 (Dako 22C3). NGS (FoundationOne CDx) demonstrated classic ccRCC driver alterations including biallelic VHL loss, PBRM1 frameshift deletion, BAP1 loss-of-function (associated with sarcomatoid differentiation and adverse prognosis), SETD2 frameshift, and TP53 p.R248W. CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion and MYC gain were also identified. TMB was low at 5 mut/Mb; MSS.

Case was reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board where consensus favored cytoreductive nephrectomy given IMDC intermediate-risk disease (2 factors), excellent performance status, predominant primary tumor burden, and sarcomatoid differentiation. She underwent right radical nephrectomy via open approach on [surgical date]. Operative course was notable for 650 mL estimated blood loss requiring 2 units pRBC intraoperatively. Final surgical pathology: pT3a pN1 (5/8 lymph nodes positive, largest with extranodal extension), R0 resection. PD-L1 CPS on the nephrectomy specimen was 18. No adrenal involvement. Renal vein and IVC free of tumor.

She presents today for routine post-operative oncology follow-up, approximately 5 weeks from surgery. She reports her recovery has been overall satisfactory. Wound has healed well per urology's assessment. She denies fever, chills, wound drainage, or abdominal pain. She has no new pulmonary symptoms — specifically no dyspnea at rest, no hemoptysis, and no worsening cough. Appetite has returned partially; she notes some residual fatigue consistent with surgical recovery. Bowel function has returned to baseline.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue improving, no fever, no unintentional weight loss beyond what is expected post-operatively.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest, mild exertional dyspnea on climbing stairs (baseline). No hemoptysis. No pleuritic chest pain.
- Gastrointestinal: Appetite mildly reduced, bowel function normalized. No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, or rectal bleeding.
- Genitourinary: Voiding without difficulty from remaining left kidney. No hematuria.
- Musculoskeletal: Some mild incisional discomfort, improving. No new bone pain.
- Neurological: No headache, focal neurological deficits, or cognitive changes.
- Endocrine: No symptoms of thyroid dysfunction, adrenal insufficiency.
- Dermatological: No rash.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain or palpitations. Known history of hypertension, well-controlled on amlodipine.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Hypertension — managed with amlodipine 5 mg daily
2. Stage IV clear cell RCC, right kidney, ISUP grade 3 with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, pT3a pN1 M1
3. Remote smoking history — 20 pack-years, quit approximately 15 years ago
4. S/p right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (cytoreductive)

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal LND (open, cytoreductive), approximately 5 weeks prior

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Retired schoolteacher. Lives with her husband. Non-smoker (quit 15 years ago, 20 pack-year history). Denies alcohol use. No illicit drug use. Good social support network.

FAMILY HISTORY:
No family history of renal cell carcinoma or other hereditary malignancies. No known hereditary cancer syndromes. Patient declined germline testing for BAP1 mutation at time of NGS result review.

ALLERGIES:
NKDA (no known drug allergies).

MEDICATIONS:
1. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily
2. Acetaminophen 500 mg orally PRN (post-operative pain)
3. No current oncologic therapy

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 16, O2 sat 97% on room air, Temp 36.8°C, Weight [recorded].

General: Alert and oriented, appears well, in no acute distress. Mood appropriate.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, crackles, or decreased breath sounds.
Abdomen: Midline surgical scar well-healed, no signs of wound infection or dehiscence. Abdomen soft, mildly tender at incision site. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No new palpable masses. Right flank without fullness.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness.
Neurological: Cranial nerves intact. No focal deficits. Gait steady.
Skin: No rash. No palmar or plantar changes.
Lymph nodes: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
- Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL (low; attributed to recent surgical blood loss and intraoperative transfusion)
- WBC: 6.8 x10^9/L (normal)
- Platelets: 218 x10^9/L (normal)
- Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL (up from pre-operative baseline of 0.9 mg/dL, expected post-right nephrectomy; eGFR ~45-50 mL/min/1.73m2)
- BUN: 22 mg/dL
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
- AST/ALT: within normal limits
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
- Albumin: 3.7 g/dL
- LDH: 198 U/L (slightly above upper limit of normal)
- TSH: 2.3 mIU/L (normal; baseline)
- Urinalysis from contralateral kidney: no significant proteinuria, no hematuria

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
Most recent cross-sectional imaging (staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) performed at time of diagnosis confirmed bilateral pulmonary metastases with the dominant lesion in the right lower lobe measuring 1.8 x 1.5 cm, along with smaller nodules in the left upper lobe (0.9 cm), right upper lobe (0.7 cm), and left lower lobe (0.6 cm). Retroperitoneal lymph nodes were surgically resected. Nephrectomy bed has not been re-imaged post-operatively at this visit; restaging imaging will be performed prior to or following initiation of systemic therapy to establish a new post-surgical baseline. No bone metastases on bone scan at diagnosis.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with Stage IV clear cell RCC (right kidney), pT3a pN1 M1, ISUP grade 3, with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, now approximately 5 weeks post right radical nephrectomy (cytoreductive, R0) with retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy resected (5/8 nodes positive). Residual metastatic disease: bilateral pulmonary nodules (not resected). IMDC intermediate risk (2 factors: ECOG performance status equivalent, time from diagnosis to systemic therapy <1 year). **HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:**
Ms. Okonkwo was initially diagnosed with stage IV ccRCC in the setting of a large 11.4 cm right renal mass with retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and bilateral pulmonary metastases. The tumor had sarcomatoid differentiation (~20%), ISUP grade 3, and PD-L1 CPS of 15-18 on surgical specimen. NGS (FoundationOne CDx) demonstrated the canonical VHL loss, as well as loss-of-function alterations in PBRM1, BAP1, and SETD2, plus a TP53 hotspot (p.R248W) — a genomic profile consistent with aggressive ccRCC, with BAP1 co-loss felt to partially explain the sarcomatoid phenotype.

She underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with RPLND approximately 18 months ago (5/8 nodes positive, extranodal extension from the largest at 2.8 cm). Following recovery, she initiated first-line ipi/nivo (Checkmate 214 regimen, ipi 1 mg/kg + nivo 3 mg/kg q3w x 4 cycles) about 6 weeks post-op. She completed 4 induction cycles with a mixed/partial response — dominant RLL lesion shrank ~44%, some other nodules stable. She was transitioned to nivo 480 mg flat dose q4w maintenance. At approximately 9 months on IO, restaging CT showed clear progression: all pulmonary nodules enlarging, two new right middle lobe nodules, and new bilateral hepatic lesions (2 in the right lobe, 0.8 and 0.6 cm). Nivo was discontinued and she was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib 60 mg daily.

Cabozantinib was dose-reduced to 40 mg at ~3 weeks for grade 2 hand-foot syndrome. Additionally, she had grade 2 diarrhea requiring brief 5-day hold and loperamide, and grade 2 hypertension requiring amlodipine uptitration to 10 mg. On 40 mg, toxicities have largely stabilized. WHOLE BODY BONE SCAN WITH SPECT

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with newly diagnosed right renal mass, concern for metastatic renal cell carcinoma. Evaluation for osseous metastatic disease prior to treatment planning.

TECHNIQUE: Following intravenous administration of approximately 25.4 mCi of Tc-99m methylene diphosphonate (MDP), whole body planar images were acquired in anterior and posterior projections at approximately 3 hours post-injection. SPECT/CT images of the lumbar spine and pelvis were obtained for lesion characterization. Images reviewed on PACS workstation.

COMPARISON: None available. This is a baseline study.

---

FINDINGS:

SKULL AND CERVICAL SPINE:
Trace heterogeneous tracer uptake in the calvarium, within normal limits for age. No focal areas of abnormally increased or photopenic uptake in the skull base or cervical spine to suggest metastatic involvement. Normal salivary gland and thyroid background activity.

THORACIC SPINE AND RIBS:
Mildly increased uptake in the mid to lower thoracic spine, most prominent at T8-T9, appearing symmetric and aligned with facet and costovertebral articulations — pattern most consistent with degenerative spondylosis. No discrete focal hot spots with morphology or distribution suspicious for osseous metastasis. The ribs show symmetric background tracer distribution bilaterally. No rib lesions identified.

LUMBAR SPINE AND SACRUM:
There is moderately increased tracer uptake diffusely throughout the lumbar spine, most prominent at L3-L4 and L4-L5 levels, consistent with multilevel degenerative disc and facet disease. SPECT/CT correlation confirms this uptake corresponds to facet joint arthropathy and endplate degenerative change without associated cortical destruction or permeative pattern. The sacrum shows symmetric, uniform uptake with no focal abnormality. Sacroiliac joints demonstrate mildly increased uptake bilaterally, symmetric, consistent with degenerative/inflammatory change.

PELVIS AND BILATERAL HIPS:
Mildly increased tracer uptake in both femoral heads and acetabular regions, bilaterally symmetric. SPECT/CT images confirm uptake corresponds to subchondral sclerosis and osteophytosis of the bilateral hip joints, consistent with moderate osteoarthritis. No discrete permeative or expansile lesion identified. Pubic symphysis uptake is within normal limits. Iliac crests appear symmetric without focal abnormality.

LONG BONES (FEMORA, TIBIAE, UPPER EXTREMITIES):
Uniform physiologic tracer uptake throughout the long bones of the lower and upper extremities. Mild symmetric uptake at both knee joint spaces likely representing degenerative change. No focal areas of abnormal increased uptake in the visualized long bones suggestive of metastatic involvement.

STERNUM AND CLAVICLES:
Normal uniform uptake in the sternum and clavicles bilaterally. Sternoclavicular joints within normal limits.

SOFT TISSUE AND RENAL ACTIVITY:
Background soft tissue activity is within expected limits. Notably, the right renal uptake appears diminished compared to the left, consistent with the known large renal mass occupying the right kidney. Left kidney demonstrates normal physiologic tracer excretion. Bladder activity is present and appropriate for timing of acquisition.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No scintigraphic evidence of osseous metastatic disease. The multifocal areas of increased tracer activity identified in the spine, sacroiliac joints, and bilateral hips demonstrate a pattern and distribution consistent with degenerative joint disease and are not suspicious for metastasis.

2. Multilevel lumbar and thoracic degenerative changes as described above, most prominent at L3-L5 and correlating with expected findings in a patient of this age.

3. Moderate bilateral hip osteoarthritis.

4. Diminished right renal uptake consistent with the known large right renal mass, as described on concurrent cross-sectional imaging.

SPECIMEN TYPE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Four (4) core needle biopsy cores, right kidney mass, CT-guided.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GROSS DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Received in formalin, labeled with patient name and "right renal mass core biopsy," are four tan-white cylindrical tissue cores ranging from 1.0 to 1.5 cm in length and 0.1 to 0.15 cm in diameter. The cores have a firm consistency with focal areas of soft, friable, tan-yellow tissue. Submitted entirely in two cassettes (A1: cores 1-2; A2: cores 3-4). Representative sections taken for H&E and immunohistochemistry.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Sections show cores of renal parenchyma infiltrated by a malignant epithelial neoplasm. The dominant architectural pattern consists of nests and alveolar clusters of polygonal tumor cells separated by a rich, delicate sinusoidal vasculature characteristic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The tumor cells have abundant optically clear to finely granular cytoplasm due to dissolved lipid and glycogen, with distinct cell borders. Nuclei are markedly enlarged and irregular with prominent nucleoli that are clearly visible at 10x objective in the majority of cells, consistent with ISUP/WHO nuclear grade 3.

Mitotic figures are readily identified (estimated 5-6 per 10 high-power fields). Areas of geographic coagulative necrosis are present, comprising approximately 15-20% of the sampled cores.

A distinct component — estimated at approximately 20% of the sampled tumor area — demonstrates high-grade sarcomatoid differentiation. This component is composed of sheets and fascicles of pleomorphic, elongated spindle cells with vesicular nuclei, prominent eosinophilic nucleoli, and brisk mitotic activity (>10 mitoses per 10 HPF). Bizarre multinucleated giant cells are focally present within the sarcomatoid component. Necrosis is disproportionately concentrated in this area. The transition between the conventional clear cell component and the sarcomatoid component is abrupt in some areas and gradual in others.

In one core, fibrous tissue consistent with focally sampled perinephric fat is present, with tumor cells infiltrating between adipocytes, indicating perinephric fat invasion.

No lymphovascular invasion is definitively identified within these limited biopsy cores, though this finding is limited in sampling.

No rhabdoid differentiation identified.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The following immunohistochemical stains were performed on sections from block A1 using standard heat-induced epitope retrieval (HIER) and appropriate positive and negative controls. Controls performed satisfactorily.

  Carbonic Anhydrase IX (CA9, clone EP161):       POSITIVE — strong, diffuse membranous staining in >90% of clear cell component; reduced/patchy in sarcomatoid foci
  PAX8 (clone MRQ-50):                            POSITIVE — strong nuclear staining in clear cell component; focal weak in sarcomatoid foci
  CD10 (clone 56C6):                              POSITIVE — strong membranous/cytoplasmic staining
  Vimentin (clone V9):                            POSITIVE — diffuse cytoplasmic staining in both clear cell and sarcomatoid components
  CK7 (clone OV-TL 12/30):                        NEGATIVE — no immunoreactivity
  TFE3 (clone MRQ-37):                            NEGATIVE — no nuclear immunoreactivity (excludes Xp11.2 translocation RCC)
  SOX10:                                          NEGATIVE — excludes melanocytic differentiation in sarcomatoid component
  Desmin:                                         NEGATIVE — no reactivity in sarcomatoid foci
  SMA (smooth muscle actin):                      FOCAL WEAK POSITIVE — non-specific, noted in sarcomatoid stroma

PD-L1 (clone 22C3, Dako PharmDx assay):
  Combined Positive Score (CPS): 15
    — Tumor cell membranous PD-L1 staining: approximately 8% of viable tumor cells positive
    — Immune cell PD-L1 staining (tumor-associated macrophages and lymphocytes): moderate infiltrate, contributing substantially to the CPS
    — Scoring performed by a board-certified pathologist; interpretation: PD-L1 CPS 15 (positive, intermediate expression)

The immunophenotype (CA9+, PAX8+, CD10+, vimentin+, CK7-, TFE3-) is consistent with clear cell renal cell carcinoma and excludes papillary RCC, chromophobe RCC, and Xp11.2 translocation-associated RCC.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
RIGHT KIDNEY, MASS — CT-GUIDED CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY:

  RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, CLEAR CELL TYPE
    - ISUP/WHO Nuclear Grade: 3 (prominent nucleoli visible at 10x objective)
    - Sarcomatoid differentiation: PRESENT, estimated 20% of sampled tumor
    - Necrosis: PRESENT (geographic coagulative necrosis, ~15-20% of sampled cores)
    - Perinephric fat involvement: PRESENT (focal, in one biopsy core)
    - Rhabdoid differentiation: Absent

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The morphologic and immunohistochemical findings are diagnostic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), ISUP/WHO grade 3, with a significant (approximately 20%) sarcomatoid component. Sarcomatoid differentiation in ccRCC is a recognized adverse prognostic feature associated with aggressive behavior, resistance to VEGFR-targeted therapy, and potentially enhanced response to immune checkpoint inhibitor-based regimens. The presence of perinephric fat invasion in one of the four cores is noted and should be correlated with the imaging and surgical findings. The PD-L1 CPS of 15 by the 22C3 assay is provided for informational purposes. Correlation with final surgical pathology at nephrectomy (if performed) is recommended to fully assess pathologic stage, margin status, and extent of sarcomatoid differentiation.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ACTIONABILITY SUMMARY — TIER CLASSIFICATION
(OncoKB / AMP-ACMG Tiering Framework)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TIER I — FDA-Approved Indications in This Tumor Type:
  • VHL biallelic loss (p.R200* + 3p LOH): Supports relevance of HIF-2α
    inhibition. Belzutifan (Welireg) is FDA-approved for VHL-mutated ccRCC
    (VHL disease-associated tumors and sporadic VHL-altered ccRCC in certain
    contexts). Relevant if prior therapies have been exhausted.
  • PD-L1 expression (CPS 15, see pathology) combined with MSS/low-TMB
    profile: consistent with immunotherapy-eligible disease (nivolumab,
    ipilimumab/nivolumab, pembrolizumab/axitinib — all FDA-approved 1L for
    advanced ccRCC regardless of PD-L1 status in this histology).

TIER II — Investigational / Emerging Biomarker Relevance:
  • BAP1 loss: Associated with sarcomatoid differentiation and poor prognosis;
    may predict enhanced benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitor combinations.
    EZH2 inhibitor activity hypothesized (H3K27 methylation dysregulation
    secondary to BAP1/ASXL loss — preclinical rationale). No approved
    indication.
  • PBRM1 loss: May identify tumors with preferential benefit from immune
    checkpoint inhibitor therapy over VEGFR TKI monotherapy (retrospective
    data from JAVELIN Renal 101, CheckMate 214 exploratory analyses).
  • CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion: CDK4/6 inhibitor sensitization rationale
    (investigational in RCC); currently no FDA-approved indication in this
    context.
  • High genomic LOH (23%): Under investigation as predictive biomarker for
    PARP inhibitor sensitivity in HRD-associated solid tumors; not established
    in ccRCC.
  • TP53 p.R248W (gain-of-function): PRIMA-1MET/APR-246 and other p53
    reactivation strategies — investigational only; no approved indication
    in RCC.
  • MYC gain: No direct targetable option currently; relevant to prognosis.

TIER III — VUS (No Current Clinical Actionability):
  • ARID1A p.S1235L, KDM5C p.R726Q, KMT2C p.R668H — as described above.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GERMLINE CONSIDERATIONS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
The identified BAP1 p.W136* nonsense mutation warrants germline evaluation given
the established BAP1 Tumor Predisposition Syndrome (BAP1-TPDS), which confers
hereditary susceptibility to uveal melanoma, malignant mesothelioma, cutaneous
melanoma, and clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The allele frequency of 52% is
consistent with either a somatic mutation with LOH or a germline mutation;
distinction requires germline (blood) testing. Genetic counseling referral is
strongly recommended. Patient has been informed; germline follow-up deferred per
patient preference at time of reporting.

Note: This assay is performed on tumor tissue and is not validated for germline
variant detection. A dedicated germline assay (blood-based) is required for
definitive germline assessment.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERPRETIVE SUMMARY
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Comprehensive genomic profiling of this right renal mass core needle biopsy
demonstrates a genomic landscape highly characteristic of aggressive clear cell
renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation. The canonical 3p loss
coupled with somatic inactivating mutations in all four major 3p tumor suppressors
(VHL, PBRM1, BAP1, SETD2) confirms biallelic inactivation of the entire 3p21
tumor suppressor cluster — a convergent genomic event seen in a subset of
high-risk ccRCC. The additional presence of TP53 p.R248W gain-of-function,
CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, and MYC copy gain collectively describe a highly
genomically unstable tumor consistent with the observed sarcomatoid histologic
transformation (estimated 20% of tumor volume on biopsy) and high nuclear grade.

The low TMB (5 mut/Mb) and MSS status indicate that TMB-high or MSI-H directed
therapies (e.g., pembrolizumab for TMB-high or MSI-H per tissue-agnostic
approvals) are not applicable. However, the genomic profile — particularly PBRM1
loss, BAP1 loss, and high PD-L1 expression (CPS 15–18) — is consistent with
immune-inflamed biology that supports consideration of immune checkpoint
combination strategies. The absence of MTOR pathway mutations (TSC1, TSC2, MTOR
wild-type) does not favor mTOR inhibitor–based regimens as a biologically driven
choice. The absence of actionable fusions or NTRK/ALK/ROS1 alterations precludes
targeted fusion-driven therapy.

Belzutifan (HIF-2α inhibitor, Welireg) may represent a rational later-line option
given confirmed biallelic VHL loss, per its mechanism of action and growing
evidence base in VHL-altered ccRCC.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TECHNICAL METHODOLOGY
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FoundationOne CDx is a next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic device
that uses DNA extracted from FFPE tumor tissue specimens to detect substitutions,
insertion-deletions (indels), copy number alterations, and select rearrangements
in 324 genes, as well as genomic signatures including TMB and MSI. The test is
performed at Foundation Medicine, Inc. (Cambridge, MA), a CAP-accredited and
CLIA-certified laboratory. RNA sequencing was performed for fusion detection.
Genomic LOH was calculated using the GenomicLOH algorithm incorporating SNP
heterozygosity calls across the evaluable genome. Tumor content was estimated
by a board-certified pathologist on H&E-stained sections of the submitted block
prior to macrodissection.

All identified variants were reviewed and classified by a board-certified molecular
pathologist. This report is intended to supplement, and should be interpreted
alongside, the clinical and pathologic findings.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old retired schoolteacher with a new diagnosis of Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) who is presenting to the GU Oncology multidisciplinary tumor board for initial treatment recommendations. Her diagnosis was established following evaluation of an incidentally noted right flank discomfort and constitutional symptoms over the preceding several months. Cross-sectional imaging revealed a large heterogeneous right renal mass measuring approximately 11.4 x 9.7 x 10.2 cm arising from the upper and mid poles of the right kidney, with central necrosis and extension into the perinephric fat. CT imaging of the chest demonstrated multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, the largest measuring 1.8 cm in the right lower lobe, with additional nodules scattered throughout both lungs. There was also radiographically apparent retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy with the largest node measuring 2.8 x 2.1 cm at the level of the renal hilum. A bone scan was performed and was negative for osseous metastases. IVC and right adrenal gland were uninvolved on imaging.

A CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of the right renal mass was performed and confirmed renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type, ISUP/WHO grade 3, with approximately 20% sarcomatoid differentiation. Immunohistochemistry was consistent with ccRCC: CA9+, PAX8+, vimentin+, CD10+, CK7-. PD-L1 by CPS (Dako 22C3) was 15. Molecular profiling by FoundationOne CDx was completed and demonstrated a genomic profile consistent with high-risk ccRCC: VHL p.R200* (biallelic loss), PBRM1 frameshift loss-of-function, BAP1 p.W136* (associated with sarcomatoid features), SETD2 frameshift, and TP53 p.R248W (hotspot, associated with sarcomatoid phenotype). Chromosome 3p loss was the dominant copy number alteration, along with MYC gain, and homozygous deletion of CDKN2A/B. TMB was low (5 mut/Mb) and microsatellite stable. No mTOR pathway alterations. No actionable fusions.

Final staging per AJCC 8th edition: pT3a Nx M1 (radiographic N1; M1 bilateral pulmonary metastases), Stage IV. IMDC risk score: Intermediate (2 risk factors — ECOG 1 approximating KPS <80%, and systemic therapy to be initiated within 1 year of diagnosis). She has been seen jointly today by medical oncology, urology, and radiation oncology.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with newly diagnosed Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma, ISUP grade 3, with notable sarcomatoid differentiation (~20%), involving the right kidney with perinephric extension, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary metastases. IMDC intermediate risk (2 factors). Molecular profile: VHL-mutant/biallelic, PBRM1 loss, BAP1 loss, SETD2 loss, TP53 hotspot mutation; consistent with aggressive biology. PD-L1 CPS 15-18.

The case was presented at the GU Multidisciplinary Tumor Board today with representation from medical oncology, urology, and radiation oncology. The following recommendations were reached by consensus:

1. CYTOREDUCTIVE NEPHRECTOMY (CN):
The board recommends proceeding with upfront cytoreductive nephrectomy prior to systemic therapy. Rationale: (a) IMDC intermediate-risk status — the survival benefit of CN is more established in this population relative to poor-risk disease; (b) the primary tumor constitutes the predominant burden of disease (11.4 cm mass with nodal involvement) while pulmonary metastases are relatively limited; (c) the presence of 20% sarcomatoid differentiation makes it important to have ample surgical tissue for full pathologic characterization and any additional molecular testing; (d) the patient's ECOG PS 1 and otherwise good organ function support surgical candidacy; (e) the right renal vein and IVC are uninvolved, making R0 resection feasible. Right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection via open approach is planned. Urology to schedule.

2. FIRST-LINE SYSTEMIC THERAPY (post-surgical recovery):
Following appropriate surgical recovery (typically 4-6 weeks), the board recommends first-line systemic therapy with ipi 1 mg/kg IV plus nivo 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles (Checkmate 214 regimen), followed by nivo 480 mg flat dose IV every 4 weeks as maintenance. Rationale: (a) IO doublet superiority over Sutent in intermediate/poor-risk ccRCC per Checkmate 214 long-term data; (b) sarcomatoid differentiation is an established biomarker of enhanced benefit from IO-based regimens — pooled analyses and dedicated sarcomatoid subgroup data from Checkmate 214 and IMmotion151 demonstrate improved ORR, PFS, and OS in sarcomatoid RCC treated with IO combinations; (c) PD-L1 CPS 15 on biopsy specimen supports IO responsiveness; (d) BAP1 and TP53 co-mutations associated with sarcomatoid phenotype may further drive immunogenic tumor biology. Alternative regimens discussed (pembro + axitinib per KEYNOTE-426; Keytruda + lenva per CLEAR trial) but ipi/nivo selected given sarcomatoid histology and level of evidence in this subgroup.

   - Patient was counseled extensively on immune-related adverse events (irAEs) including but not limited to: immune colitis/diarrhea, immune hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies (thyroid, adrenal, pituitary), nephritis, myocarditis, and dermatologic toxicity. Steroid taper protocols and when to contact the clinic reviewed.
   - Given penicillin allergy, antibiotic prophylaxis (if required peri-operatively) to be discussed with surgical team.
   - Patient verbalized understanding of treatment rationale, risks, and alternatives. She is agreeable to proceed.

3. RADIATION ONCOLOGY:
No role for upfront radiation therapy identified. Will re-evaluate if oligoprogressive or symptomatic disease arises.

4. SUPPORTIVE CARE / ANCILLARY:
   - Renal function monitoring post-nephrectomy; anticipated creatinine rise.
   - Nutritional assessment referral given 8-lb weight loss.
   - CBC and CMP within 2 weeks of initiating systemic therapy.
   - Consider baseline thyroid function, LFTs, glucose, and CBC prior to ipi/nivo start.
   - Genetic counseling: the NGS result identifies a BAP1 loss-of-function alteration. BAP1 germline mutations confer susceptibility to mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, cutaneous melanoma, and ccRCC. Germline testing was discussed with the patient today; she has elected to defer at this time but the discussion was documented and she will reconsider at future visit.

5. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Patient to return to medical oncology approximately 5 weeks post-nephrectomy for pre-treatment assessment and initiation of ipi/nivo.
   - Urology to coordinate surgical scheduling.
   - All treating teams to communicate via the GU tumor board portal.

GROSS DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Part A — Right Kidney with Perirenal Fat:

Received fresh, then fixed in formalin, is a right radical nephrectomy specimen consisting of a kidney with attached perirenal adipose tissue and a segment of proximal ureter. The specimen in aggregate measures 14.5 x 11.8 x 9.0 cm and weighs 710 grams (unfixed). The external surface of the perirenal fat is inked black. The ureter is identified, measures 2.8 cm in length and 0.5 cm in diameter, and is submitted entirely as a shave margin (Section A1).

The kidney is bivalved along its longitudinal axis. The renal capsule is intact over the non-neoplastic kidney but is effaced and locally disrupted by the tumor mass. A dominant, poorly circumscribed neoplasm is identified occupying the upper and mid poles of the kidney, measuring 11.4 cm in greatest dimension (11.4 x 9.2 x 8.8 cm in three orthogonal dimensions). The cut surface of the tumor is heterogeneous, with a bright yellow-orange to tan periphery of viable-appearing solid tumor interspersed with irregular zones of central chalky-white and hemorrhagic necrosis accounting for approximately 30% of the total tumor area. Small foci of firmer, white-gray tissue are noted at the periphery of the neoplasm, morphologically consistent with areas of sarcomatoid transformation identified in the prior biopsy.

The tumor extends grossly into the perirenal adipose tissue. Surgical margins are assessed as follows:
- Perinephric fat (radial soft tissue margin): focally involved by tumor at one pole (inked black; minimal clearance, less than 1 mm at the closest point, though no frank transection of tumor is identified)
- Gerota's fascia: submitted separately; appears grossly intact
- Renal sinus fat: the tumor extends into and involves the renal sinus fat at the hilum
- Renal vein margin: the renal vein stump is present, measures 1.4 cm in circumference at the staple line; serial sectioning reveals no gross tumor within the renal vein lumen; margin submitted as a shave section (Section A2)
- Ureteral margin: see A1 above

The non-neoplastic renal parenchyma is grossly unremarkable. The cortex is tan-pink. No additional discrete masses identified.

Representative sections submitted:
  A1 — Ureteral shave margin
  A2 — Renal vein shave margin
  A3-A4 — Tumor with closest perirenal fat margin (inked)
  A5 — Tumor with extension into perinephric fat
  A6 — Tumor center, areas of necrosis
  A7 — Sarcomatoid-appearing gross region at tumor periphery
  A8 — Tumor with renal sinus fat involvement
  A9 — Tumor-renal parenchyma interface
  A10 — Non-neoplastic kidney
  A11 — Renal capsule over tumor
  A12 — Additional viable tumor, yellow-tan region

Part B — Retroperitoneal Lymph Nodes:

Received in formalin in three labeled containers:
  B1 (Hilar): Multiple fragments of fibroadipose tissue in aggregate measuring 5.4 x 3.8 x 1.7 cm, with 3 grossly identifiable discrete lymph nodes, the largest measuring 2.8 cm in greatest dimension. The largest node has a pale tan-gray cut surface with focal areas of necrosis consistent with metastatic involvement. Entirely submitted (3 cassettes).
  B2 (Paraaortic superior): Fibroadipose tissue measuring 4.2 x 2.9 x 1.5 cm with 2 grossly identifiable lymph nodes, the larger measuring 1.9 cm. Entire tissue submitted (2 cassettes).
  B3 (Paraaortic inferior): Fibroadipose tissue measuring 3.6 x 2.1 x 1.2 cm with 3 grossly identifiable lymph nodes, all under 1.0 cm in greatest dimension. Entire tissue submitted (2 cassettes).

Total nodes submitted from Part B: at least 8 nodes identified grossly; complete histologic assessment below.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Part A — Right Kidney:

Tumor: The primary renal neoplasm is composed predominantly of cells with abundant optically clear cytoplasm, centrally placed nuclei with prominent nucleoli, arranged in nests, alveoli, and trabeculae supported by a delicate arborizing sinusoidal vascular network, classic for clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The nuclear grade is assessed as ISUP/WHO Grade 3, defined by the presence of prominent nucleoli visible at 10x magnification in the predominant tumor component, with occasional cells with more extreme nuclear enlargement approaching grade 4 features at the sarcomatoid interface.

Sarcomatoid differentiation is identified and constitutes approximately 20% of the viable neoplasm. The sarcomatoid component is characterized by high-grade spindle cell morphology with elongated, pleomorphic nuclei, coarse chromatin, conspicuous nucleoli, and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm arranged in fascicles and storiform patterns. Brisk mitotic activity is present in the sarcomatoid component (up to 7 mitoses per 10 high-power fields, including atypical forms). Rhabdoid differentiation is not identified.

Necrosis: Geographic and coagulative tumor necrosis is present, estimated at 30% of the sampled tumor area.

Extension into perirenal fat: Present. Irregular tongues of tumor with desmoplastic stroma extend through the renal capsule into the perinephric adipose tissue.

Renal sinus fat involvement: Present. Tumor extends along vascular channels and fibrous septa into the renal sinus adipose tissue at the hilum.

Gerota's fascia: Sections of Gerota's fascia are submitted; no definitive tumor involves the deep surface of Gerota's fascia. The fascia appears intact on available sections.

Renal vein (Part A2): Serial sections of the renal vein shave margin demonstrate no intravascular tumor thrombus. The vein wall is unremarkable. Renal vein margin: NEGATIVE.

Ureteral margin (Part A1): Sections show normal urothelium lining with no atypia and no carcinoma. Ureteral margin: NEGATIVE.

Perinephric fat margin: The tumor approaches the inked perirenal fat margin closely (less than 1 mm at the closest point) but no frank transection of tumor at the inked margin is identified. This represents an R0 resection with a focally close margin.

Non-neoplastic kidney (Part A10): The adjacent renal parenchyma shows mild cortical simplification, globally sclerosed glomeruli (approximately 15%), and mild interstitial fibrosis consistent with background nephrosclerosis in the setting of hypertension. No additional neoplasms identified.

Part B — Lymph Nodes:

Of the retroperitoneal lymph nodes submitted, a total of 8 lymph nodes are identified histologically after complete processing and sectioning:

  B1 (Hilar): 3 of 3 nodes positive for metastatic renal cell carcinoma. The largest positive node measures 2.8 cm in greatest dimension and demonstrates extranodal extension: metastatic tumor penetrates through the lymph node capsule into the perinodal adipose tissue. The metastatic deposits consist of clear cell RCC, consistent with the primary tumor. The sarcomatoid component is also identified within the metastatic deposits in the largest node.

  B2 (Paraaortic superior): 2 of 2 nodes positive for metastatic renal cell carcinoma. Metastatic clear cell RCC with focal necrosis. No extranodal extension.

  B3 (Paraaortic inferior): 0 of 3 nodes positive (3 nodes with reactive follicular hyperplasia; no carcinoma identified).

Summary: 5 of 8 retroperitoneal lymph nodes positive for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (5/8). Extranodal extension present (largest hilar node).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANCILLARY STUDIES — IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Immunohistochemistry was performed on representative sections of the primary renal tumor (surgical specimen) and correlated with the prior biopsy immunoprofile. Results confirmed on CONFIRM platform using standard positive and negative controls.

  CA9 (Carbonic Anhydrase IX, clone EP161):   Diffuse strong membranous positivity (3+, >95% of clear cell component). Reduced/absent in sarcomatoid foci.
  PAX8 (clone MRQ-50):                         Diffuse nuclear positivity in both clear cell and sarcomatoid components.
  Vimentin (clone V9):                          Diffuse strong cytoplasmic positivity.
  CD10 (clone 56C6):                            Diffuse membranous/cytoplasmic positivity.
  CK7 (clone OV-TL 12/30):                     Negative in clear cell component; rare positive cells in sarcomatoid areas.
  CK AE1/AE3 (broad-spectrum):                 Focal weak positivity in sarcomatoid component (~10% cells); diffuse in clear cell component.
  EMA (clone E29):                              Focal positive.
  TFE3 (clone MRQ-37):                          Negative (no translocation-associated overexpression).
  CAIX:                                         See CA9 above (same clone used).
  SOX10:                                        Negative (excludes sarcomatoid dedifferentiation with melanocytic/nerve sheath phenotype).
  Desmin:                                       Negative.
  SMA (alpha-smooth muscle actin):              Focal positive in spindled areas (consistent with myofibroblastic stroma, not diagnostic of leiomyosarcomatoid differentiation).

PD-L1 Immunohistochemistry:
  Assay:           Dako PD-L1 IHC 22C3 pharmDx (FDA-approved companion diagnostic assay)
  Scoring:         Combined Positive Score (CPS) methodology
  Result:          CPS = 18
  Interpretation:  PD-L1 positive (CPS ≥1). Tumor cell PD-L1 expression predominantly in the clear cell component (TPS approximately 25-30%); immune cells strongly positive contributing to the overall CPS score. PD-L1 expression notably enriched at the interface of sarcomatoid and clear cell components.

Note: Ki-67 proliferative index (clone MIB-1): approximately 35% in the high-grade/sarcomatoid component; approximately 12% in the conventional clear cell areas.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DIAGNOSIS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
A. RIGHT KIDNEY, RADICAL NEPHRECTOMY:
   - RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, CLEAR CELL TYPE
     ISUP/WHO NUCLEAR GRADE: 3
     SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION: PRESENT (~20% of viable tumor)
     TUMOR SIZE: 11.4 cm greatest dimension
     TUMOR NECROSIS: PRESENT (approximately 30% of tumor area)
     LYMPHOVASCULAR INVASION: PRESENT (identified in peritumoral vascular channels)
     PERINEPHRIC FAT INVASION: PRESENT
     RENAL SINUS FAT INVASION: PRESENT
     GEROTA'S FASCIA: NOT INVOLVED (intact on available sections)
     RENAL VEIN MARGIN: NEGATIVE FOR TUMOR
     URETERAL MARGIN: NEGATIVE FOR CARCINOMA
     PERIRENAL SOFT TISSUE MARGIN: FOCALLY CLOSE (<1 mm) BUT NEGATIVE (R0)
     ADRENAL GLAND: NOT SUBMITTED (retained intraoperatively)

B. RETROPERITONEAL LYMPH NODES:
   - METASTATIC CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA
     5 OF 8 LYMPH NODES POSITIVE (5/8)
     LARGEST POSITIVE NODE: 2.8 cm WITH EXTRANODAL EXTENSION
     LOCATION: HILAR (3/3) AND PARAAORTIC SUPERIOR (2/2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PATHOLOGIC STAGING (AJCC 8th Edition, Kidney)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Primary Tumor (pT):    pT3a — Tumor extends into perinephric fat and/or renal sinus fat but does not extend beyond Gerota's fascia and does not extend into the ipsilateral adrenal gland
  Regional Lymph Nodes:  pN1 — Metastasis in regional lymph node(s)
  Distant Metastasis:    pM1 — Distant metastasis present (bilateral pulmonary metastases confirmed on clinical imaging; histologic confirmation of distant mets not performed at this time)
  Overall Stage:         Stage IV (pT3a pN1 pM1)

  Histologic Type:       Renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type (8310/3)
  Histologic Grade:      ISUP/WHO Grade 3
  Surgical Margins:      R0 (negative, focally close at perirenal fat margin)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The morphologic and immunophenotypic features are diagnostic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation. The immunoprofile (CA9+, PAX8+, CD10+, vimentin+, CK7-, TFE3-) is consistent with and confirms the clear cell RCC subtype. The sarcomatoid transformation (approximately 20% of viable tumor) is defined by the presence of malignant spindle cells with high-grade nuclear features as described above, and is a recognized adverse prognostic feature associated with aggressive clinical behavior, chemoresistance, and inferior outcomes with standard targeted therapies. The high PD-L1 CPS (18) is consistent with the prior biopsy result (CPS 15) and with the known association between sarcomatoid differentiation and upregulated PD-L1 expression in ccRCC.

Lymphrovascular invasion is identified, and lymph node metastases are extensive (5/8 nodes), including extranodal extension from the largest hilar node. These findings, in conjunction with the established distant metastatic disease, are consistent with a high metastatic burden.

The non-neoplastic kidney shows background nephrosclerotic changes consistent with long-standing hypertension.

This report has been reviewed by a second pathologist. Results are consistent with the pre-operative core biopsy diagnosis.
